title: The Almanack of MM — Mental Model Quiz: Part I Judgment
source: The Almanack of MM by Eric Jorgenson (2020)

questions.question: MM says 'clear thinker' is a better compliment than 'smart.' What does he mean, and why does fluency with advanced concepts actually signal a lack of understanding?
questions.short_answer: Real knowledge is built from first principles that you can rederive on demand. Fluency with complex jargon without being able to trace it back to fundamentals is just memorization — which collapses under pressure.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's distinction cuts against the common signal of intelligence: using sophisticated vocabulary and advanced frameworks. He argues that if you truly understand something, you can reconstruct it from the ground up, the way Feynman built all of precalculus in three pages starting from counting. Advanced concepts in any field are also the least proven — they exist largely to signal insider status rather than to transmit durable truth. The test of real knowledge is not whether you can recall a concept, but whether you can rederive it from simpler premises when you need it. Someone who cannot do this is only memorizing — their understanding is shallow and brittle. This is why MM prefers mastering the basics deeply over accumulating a wide inventory of complex ideas he cannot independently verify.
questions.section: Building Judgment — How to Think Clearly

questions.question: MM defines a specific relationship between desire, ego, and the ability to perceive reality accurately. What is that relationship, and what does it reveal about the nature of suffering?
questions.short_answer: Desire and ego act as filters that distort perception — we see what we wish were true rather than what is. Suffering, paradoxically, is the moment when that filter is forcibly removed and reality becomes undeniable.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM frames the 'monkey mind' as a system that generates emotionally pre-packaged responses based on how we think the world should be, rather than how it is. The stronger our desire for a particular outcome, the harder we work — unconsciously — to screen out contradicting evidence. This is why a friend can see clearly that your relationship is failing while you cannot: they have no stake, so no distortion. The disturbing implication is that clarity requires reducing the size of your ego and the intensity of your desires, not acquiring more information. Suffering enters this model as an unlikely ally: pain is the moment when reality can no longer be denied, making it a prerequisite for progress. You can only change what you're willing to see.
questions.section: Building Judgment — How to Think Clearly

questions.question: Why does MM insist on scheduling empty, unscheduled time each week, and what does this reveal about the preconditions for good judgment?
questions.short_answer: Creative and strategic thinking cannot happen under constant busyness — good ideas emerge only from boredom, which requires deliberately protecting empty space in the calendar.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM treats cognitive slack as a non-negotiable input, not a luxury. When every hour is filled with meetings and reactive tasks, the brain is operating in an execution mode that crowds out the reflective thinking required for good decisions. The great ideas, he says, come only after boredom — not during stress or urgency. This is a direct challenge to the cultural equation of busyness with productivity. MM's practical recommendation — budget two days a week of unstructured time so you end up with at least one — is itself a meta-example of clear thinking: he applies the principle of margin and overshoot to his own schedule design. The deeper insight is that judgment is not a skill you apply when needed; it requires a prior state of mental openness that only idleness can create.
questions.section: Building Judgment — How to Think Clearly

questions.question: MM distinguishes a true contrarian from someone who reflexively objects. What is the actual definition of contrarianism, and why does it require reasoning from the ground up?
questions.short_answer: A contrarian is not someone who defaults to disagreement — that is just conformism in reverse. True contrarians reason independently from first principles and resist social pressure to conform, even when that pressure comes from their own tribe.
questions.elaborated_answer: The key word in MM's formulation is 'ground up.' Reflexive objection is still a reaction to external signals — you're still letting the crowd define your position by negating it. Genuine contrarianism means forming your view before consulting consensus, through your own chain of reasoning from the basics. This is why MM links contrarianism to clear thinking: both require the same foundational skill of building independently rather than inheriting a position from a package deal. He also notes that optimistic contrarians — those who hold heterodox views while remaining constructive and forward-looking — are the rarest type of all, because most people who deviate from consensus do so out of cynicism, which is itself an easy, socially tolerated form of conformity.
questions.section: Building Judgment — How to Think Clearly

questions.principle: Shed Your Identity to See Reality
questions.question: MM argues that beliefs acquired as a 'package deal' — Democrat, Catholic, libertarian — should be treated with suspicion. What is the specific cognitive danger of packaged belief systems?
questions.short_answer: When you adopt an identity label, you inherit a bundle of positions you have not individually evaluated. You then feel compelled to defend those positions, which means your reasoning serves your identity rather than the truth.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM uses his own experience with libertarianism as the example: once he identified as libertarian, he found himself defending positions he had never independently reasoned through because they were part of the canon. The tell, he says, is when all your beliefs line up into neat, internally consistent bundles — that is a sign that they were pre-packaged rather than independently derived, because reality is messy and independent reasoning almost never produces a perfectly coherent ideological system. The cognitive trap is that identity creates motivated reasoning: you are no longer asking 'what is true?' but 'what must I believe to remain who I am?' This is why MM's prescription is radical: do not self-identify on almost any level. To be honest, speak without identity.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Shed Your Identity to See Reality

questions.principle: Shed Your Identity to See Reality
questions.question: MM treats habits and identity as a bundle that needs periodic auditing. What is the process he recommends for distinguishing useful habits from identity artifacts, and what is at stake if you skip that audit?
questions.short_answer: The audit means tracing each habit back to its origin and asking whether it still serves your current goals — because many habits formed in early childhood for reasons that no longer apply but get mistaken for 'who you are.'
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's framework is that habits are initially adaptive: you cannot solve every problem from scratch every time. But over time, habits calcify into identity — 'This is just how I am' — and that identity makes them feel permanent and unquestionable. The problem is that a habit formed by a toddler seeking parental attention is not the same as a deliberate strategy for adult success. By attaching identity to habits, people foreclose the possibility of reassessing them. MM's prescription is unconditioning: deliberately separating the habit from the identity wrapper, evaluating it on its current merits, and discarding it if it no longer serves. The cost of skipping this audit is living out patterns shaped by accidents of history rather than conscious design — a kind of cognitive path dependency.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Shed Your Identity to See Reality

questions.principle: Shed Your Identity to See Reality
questions.question: MM says that the beliefs our own identity and local tribe most strongly reject are the ones most likely to be real. Why is social rejection from your own tribe actually evidence in favor of a contrarian belief?
questions.short_answer: If a belief were false or harmless, your tribe would either ignore it or disprove it easily. Strong tribal rejection signals the belief is threatening to shared assumptions — which is precisely where entrenched blind spots tend to live.
questions.elaborated_answer: This is one of MM's most counterintuitive epistemic rules. The logic runs as follows: your tribe shares a set of identity-protecting beliefs. A claim that your own tribe rejects with unusual intensity has passed through the harshest possible filter — it has survived being scrutinized by people who are strongly motivated to find it false. Generic falsehoods get debunked easily. It is the truths that threaten identity — that reveal something uncomfortable about the group — that get the most furious pushback. MM is effectively inverting the usual credibility signal: instead of taking tribal consensus as evidence of truth, he takes concentrated tribal hostility as weak evidence of truth. This squares with his broader framework: identity distorts perception, so the perceptions most distorted by identity are precisely those your tribe defends most aggressively.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Shed Your Identity to See Reality

questions.question: MM claims that someone who is right 80% of the time rather than 70% of the time is worth hundreds of times more in the market. What structural feature of modern leverage explains this extreme, nonlinear payoff?
questions.short_answer: Modern technology and large workforces amplify every decision across enormous amounts of capital and people, so a marginal improvement in accuracy compounds multiplicatively rather than linearly through the scale of the operation.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM uses the example of managing a $1 billion portfolio: being right 10% more often generates $100 million of value per call — not $100 million per year, but per decision. This is a leverage effect: the same judgment, applied at scale, produces outcomes that grow faster than the underlying improvement in accuracy. In pre-modern contexts, better judgment improved your life proportionally. In modern contexts — where a single product decision or investment call affects millions of users or dollars — even tiny improvements in decision quality generate orders-of-magnitude differences in outcomes. This is why MM treats decision-making as the foundational skill above all others, and why he views the effort to reduce cognitive biases as among the highest-return investments a person can make. The market does not pay linearly for accuracy; it pays exponentially.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Learn the Skills of Decision-Making

questions.question: MM's rule 'If you can't decide, the answer is no' seems like simple advice but rests on a specific structural argument about modern life. What is that argument?
questions.short_answer: We evolved in tribes of 150 people with few options, but now live in a world of billions of people and near-infinite choices — which means hesitation reveals that an option is not actually compelling enough to justify the long time-lock it requires.
questions.elaborated_answer: The logic is probabilistic and opportunity-cost-based. Long-duration decisions — starting a company, choosing a partner, moving to a city — lock you in for years or decades. In a world with seven billion connected people and hundreds of thousands of career paths, the number of options is vastly greater than our evolved intuition can process. If you are genuinely uncertain — to the point of building a pros-and-cons spreadsheet — it means the option has not crossed the threshold of obviously-right. Given the duration of the commitment and the abundance of alternatives, the expected value of waiting for something that does cross that threshold is higher than taking something that merely 'might be okay.' Hesitation in a world of abundant options is not a decision problem; it is already the answer.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Learn the Skills of Decision-Making

questions.question: MM's 'Run Uphill' heuristic says that when two options are roughly equal, choose the one with more short-term pain. What does this reveal about how the brain systematically misprices the two paths?
questions.short_answer: When paths appear equal, the brain is actually overweighting short-term comfort on one side — meaning the painful path almost certainly has more long-term gain, and the brain's conflict-avoidance is hiding that asymmetry.
questions.elaborated_answer: The heuristic exploits a known asymmetry in human cognition: we discount future rewards steeply and overweight immediate costs. If two choices feel 'roughly equal,' it is because the brain has already inflated the attractiveness of the easier short-term path and suppressed the appeal of the harder one. The paths are not actually equal — the brain is presenting a distorted ledger. The correct adjustment is not to trust that ledger but to invert it: systematically favor the option with short-term pain, because the law of compound interest means long-term gain grows disproportionately over time. MM applies this across domains: working out, reading difficult books, acknowledging business failures publicly. In each case, accepting near-term discomfort is the price of compounding future benefits that conflict-avoidance would otherwise forfeit.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Learn the Skills of Decision-Making

questions.question: MM contrasts using 'X happened in the past, therefore X will happen in the future' with using mental models. Why is the first approach so inadequate, and what do mental models actually do that raw experience cannot?
questions.short_answer: Pattern-matching from past events is too anchored to the specific circumstances of that event. Mental models extract the underlying principle — the mechanism — and make it portable across entirely different surface-level situations.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM frames the brain as a memory-prediction machine. The naive version of prediction is essentially interpolation: seeing a situation that resembles a past event and predicting the same outcome. But most real-world situations are novel combinations of variables that do not match any past event precisely. A mental model, by contrast, captures a mechanism — how something works — and that mechanism transfers across domains. The principal-agent problem does not just explain corporate structure; it explains why you should get your own medical opinion rather than trusting an intermediary. Inversion does not just apply to business strategy; it applies to health, relationships, and personal ethics. The compressive power of mental models is what makes them worth loading into memory: they give you predictive and explanatory leverage in situations you have never encountered before.

questions.question: MM calls the principal-agent problem 'the single most fundamental problem in microeconomics.' What is the core dynamic, and why does company size directly modulate how severely it distorts behavior?
questions.short_answer: An agent acts on behalf of a principal but optimizes for their own interests rather than the principal's — and this misalignment grows as the gap between effort and personal reward widens, which scales with company size.
questions.elaborated_answer: Julius Caesar's maxim captures the essence: if you want it done right, you have to go yourself. The owner-operator — the principal — has skin in the game: success and failure accrue directly to them, creating alignment between effort and outcome. An agent acting for someone else has no such alignment; they can do a bad job, cut corners, or optimize for their own metrics without bearing the full consequences. MM's insight about company size is structural: in a small company, everyone feels like a principal — the stakes are personal and visible. As organizations grow, individuals become more agent-like because the connection between their work and outcomes diffuses across more layers. His prescription follows directly: the more tightly you can tie compensation to value created, the more you restore principal-like incentives — and the better the work gets.

questions.question: MM's mental model of inversion reframes success entirely. Instead of asking 'How do I make this work?', what does inversion prescribe — and why is that framing more reliable?
questions.short_answer: Inversion asks 'What would guarantee failure?' and then avoids those things. MM claims success is largely about not making mistakes rather than making correct positive judgments, and eliminating the wrong is more tractable than identifying the right.
questions.elaborated_answer: The standard approach to success is additive: accumulate the right skills, relationships, and moves. Inversion is subtractive: identify the clear paths to failure and remove them systematically. MM applies this because he distrusts his own ability to predict what will work — but he has much more confidence in identifying what will not work. This asymmetry is real: catastrophic errors (bad partners, high burn rates, dishonesty, ignoring early signals of failure) are more legible and more preventable than optimal positive actions, which require forecasting an inherently uncertain future. The strategy is robust under uncertainty in a way that positive prediction is not. Charlie Munger, whom MM credits heavily, uses the same approach: 'All I want to know is where I'm going to die, so I'll never go there.'

questions.question: MM says falsifiability is the most important and least understood principle for anyone claiming science on their side. How does he use this standard to critique macroeconomics specifically?
questions.short_answer: A claim is scientific only if it makes predictions that could in principle be proven wrong. Macroeconomics cannot run controlled experiments on actual economies, so its predictions can never be properly tested — meaning it cannot self-correct the way genuine sciences do.
questions.elaborated_answer: Falsifiability, as defined by Karl Popper, requires that a theory generate predictions that observable evidence could contradict. Without that property, a theory can absorb any outcome after the fact and is unfalsifiable in practice. MM's charge against macroeconomics is structural: you cannot run two versions of the US economy simultaneously to test competing hypotheses. Every macroeconomic model can be retrofitted to explain whatever happened, and proponents can always claim that the counterfactual would have been worse. This insulates macroeconomic claims from the corrective pressure that makes hard sciences self-improving over time. MM's prescription follows: weight your confidence in proportion to how falsifiable a claim is, and be proportionally skeptical of elaborate theories built on empirically untestable foundations — regardless of how mathematical or credentialed they appear.

questions.question: MM argues that 'the desire to learn is scarce' while 'the means of learning are abundant.' Why is this inversion significant, and what does it imply about where the real bottleneck to intellectual growth lies?
questions.short_answer: We live in what MM calls the age of Alexandria — every book ever written is available on demand — so access is no longer the constraint. The binding limit is cultivating genuine intrinsic motivation to learn, which cannot be faked or hacked.
questions.elaborated_answer: For most of human history, access to information was the primary bottleneck: books were scarce, expensive, and geographically locked. That bottleneck has been fully dissolved. The remaining constraint is internal: do you actually want to learn, or are you performing the appearance of learning? This reframe has practical teeth. It explains why MM's reading advice is so counterintuitive — read what you love, feel no obligation to finish anything, treat books like throwaway blog posts. The entire strategy is designed to cultivate and protect the intrinsic desire to read by removing the friction and obligation that kill it. Once that desire is genuine and habitual, even reading one to two hours per day places you in the top fraction of a percent of humans — not because the bar is high, but because almost everyone destroys their own motivation before it can compound.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Learn to Love to Read

questions.question: MM warns that well-read people can still be intellectually weak if they read 'the wrong things in the wrong order.' What specifically goes wrong, and why is the ordering of foundational reading so critical?
questions.short_answer: If your earliest reading establishes false or weakly true axioms, every subsequent idea gets filtered through and judged by that corrupted foundation — meaning volume of reading can actually compound intellectual error rather than correct it.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's argument is epistemological: your worldview is built hierarchically, with early beliefs forming the axioms on which later beliefs rest. If those axioms are false, the entire structure built atop them will be distorted, regardless of how much additional reading you do. Worse, additional reading on top of a bad foundation will mostly be evaluated by whether it confirms the existing axioms — a self-reinforcing loop. His prescription is to start with what almost no one disputes: mathematics, hard sciences, and microeconomics. These are 'solid foundations' precisely because they are falsifiable, cumulative, and self-correcting. From there he recommends originals over interpretations — Darwin before Dawkins, Adam Smith before any modern economist — because reading the source forces direct engagement rather than receiving someone else's filtered summary. The goal is a foundation rigid enough to anchor everything that follows.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Learn to Love to Read

questions.question: MM's approach to books rejects the cultural norm of finishing what you start. What is his counterargument, and how does treating 'number of books completed' as a vanity metric change reading behavior?
questions.short_answer: Most nonfiction books make a single point supported by redundant examples — once you have the core idea, finishing provides no marginal value. Tracking completions optimizes for the wrong thing and crowds out better books.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's diagnosis is that the cultural injunction to finish books — rooted in school, where assigned readings must be completed — persists into adult life as a form of obligation that distorts reading behavior. People get stuck on a book at page 332, feel guilty about not finishing, and eventually stop reading altogether rather than quit. The cure is a reframe: the only meaningful metric is whether you are absorbing new concepts with predictive power, not whether you have turned every page. Since most nonfiction is structured as one key insight plus extensive illustration, a skilled reader can often extract the core in one or two chapters and rationally stop. MM's behavior — simultaneously reading ten to twenty books, skipping freely, abandoning without guilt — is the practical expression of optimizing for insight density rather than completion rate. It is also how he recovered from Twitter-shortened attention spans to return to serious reading.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Learn to Love to Read

questions.question: MM proposes a rule for problem-solving: the older the problem, the older the solution you should seek. What is the reasoning behind this heuristic, and what kind of filter do old books apply that recent ones cannot?
questions.short_answer: A book that has survived two thousand years has been read, tested, and re-endorsed by countless generations — meaning it has survived a brutal filtering process that recent books have not yet undergone.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM applies a survivorship logic to books: longevity is itself a quality signal. A book published last year has been read by perhaps tens of thousands of people over a short window. A book read continuously for two millennia has been evaluated by millions of people across radically different cultures, circumstances, and historical moments — and kept finding new readers. That persistence is evidence that the principles inside are robust and generalize across contexts. This is especially true for old problems: how to maintain physical health, how to stay calm, how to raise a family, how to build good values. These problems have not changed in structure across human history, so solutions developed under radically different material conditions still apply. Conversely, for genuinely new problems — how to use a car, how to manage a social media following — old books are no guide, and recent expertise is appropriate.
questions.section: Building Judgment — Learn to Love to Read
